title: The Use of Handels Messiah
wikitext:
[]: The Use of Handel's Messiah             Quote Post by bobhope » Tue Oct 08, 2013 5:19 pm Why is the use of Handel's Messiah during Asuka's defeat by Arael in Ep 22 ironic? Or really just why was that song used as what it is, and what it is contemporarily interpreted as?

[]:             Quote Post by FreakyFilmFan4ever » Tue Oct 08, 2013 5:42 pm The Hallelujah chorus is only a fraction of Handel's Messiah. It's the part of the symphony that musically portrays the world ending at the end of Earth's existence before Jesus creates a new heaven and a new Earth. Basically, it plays when everything we ever knew starts tumbling down, tumbling down, tumbling down, before making way for something the spiritual entity (Jesus) wants over what humans want. Granted, for Christians like me, a new heaven and a new Earth sounds pretty cool. But it would still mean a mostly unpleasant ridding of the current heaven and the current Earth before that happens. In that sense, it's actually kind of perfect for the "Angel" destroying Asuka's mental stability.

[]:             Quote Post by Kendrix » Wed Oct 09, 2013 10:25 am ...I recently read a fanfic that offered the interesting possibility that the music is, infact, not just BGM, but in Asuka's head, frrom some childhood memory, possibly that church in the flashback of her mother's burial. Probably not the intended interpretation, but a cool idea/possebility. Ironic Music use and Asuka just come hand in hand, frachise wide: Rebuild has her butchered to a song about lasting friendship and meeting again (At first, Shinji thinks she's dead and there WONT be another meeting - they actually do meet again, but by then, Next time she and Shinji me, their friendship is pretty much out of the window), and let's not forget that scene in "Death" where the unit 02 theme in all its bombastic fanfares plays during her battles... even after we get to horrofic, violating losses.

[]:             Quote Post by Gob Hobblin » Wed Oct 09, 2013 2:00 pm The use of Handel certainly sells the 'religious' aspect of the attack. There's a connotation of Asuka receiving a 'truth' that she is unprepared and unable to take in, much like Moses looking on the back of God and being changed for it. It also has that cruel irony of the 'rape' aspect that is much more obvious in the attack. Having something religious and glorious playing over what is one of the cruelest battles in the series has that nice...what's the word? I can't describe it, but the sensation from it is one that hits right at the lobes. It was an excellent choice to play over the scene.

[]:             Quote Post by Chuckman » Wed Oct 09, 2013 2:04 pm Dissonance is the word you're looking for. The use of the music sets up a thematic contrast between her greatest suffering and the events of episode nine, when she was most happy.

[]:             Quote Post by Gob Hobblin » Wed Oct 09, 2013 2:08 pm Thank you, Chuckman! That's exactly the word.
